s: Red teak pillars; men swung 25m to snatch gold — banned after deaths
w: In a Brahmin ceremony tied to the god Shiva, teams of men once launched themselves 25 metres into the air on this swing, attempting to bite a bag of gold coins from a bamboo pole. The ritual, the Triyamphawai, stopped after fatal accidents in 1935. The 21-metre crimson structure standing in front of Wat Suthat is a 2006 replacement in teak; the original timbers are in the National Museum. Every March, at dusk, the setting sun aligns precisely through the swing's centre.
_facts: Constructed 1784 by Rama I; current teak structure completed 2006, replacing the storm-damaged original
The Triyamphawai Brahmin ceremony involved men swinging 25m to bite a bag of gold coins
Ceremony discontinued in 1935 after multiple fatal accidents
Timbers of the original swing are preserved at the Bangkok National Museum
The setting sun aligns with the swing's centre once a year, typically in early March

s: 32m standing Buddha whose topknot holds a Sri Lankan relic
w: Hidden under an expressway flyover in the northern fringe of Banglamphu, this temple holds one of Bangkok's most improbable monuments: a 32-metre standing Buddha that took from 1867 to 1927 to finish. Its inspiration was Somdej Toh, the most revered monk of the 19th century, who died at the statue's feet in 1871 while it was still being built. The golden mosaic tiles facing it are later work, but the topknot — the ushnisha — contains a genuine relic of the Buddha, gifted by Sri Lanka in 1978.
_facts: Construction of the Luang Pho To statue began 1867 and took 60 years to complete in 1927
The abbot Somdej Toh, who inspired the statue, died at its feet in 1871 during construction
The 32-metre image was refaced with Italian golden mosaic tiles in 1982 for Bangkok's 200th anniversary
The statue's topknot (ushnisha) contains a Buddha relic gifted by the Sri Lankan government in 1978
Temple dates to the Ayutthaya period; originally called Wat Rai Phrik ('vegetable fields temple')

s: Royal palace site turned spicy-southern-food alley by the river
w: The name means 'rear palace': this 300-metre lane was once the estate of Prince Anurak Devesh, nephew of Rama I, whose palace anchored the western bank opposite Thammasat University. Remnants of the palace wall still survive near the market entrance. The prince's land eventually became Bangkok's most concentrated run of southern Thai restaurants — fiercely spiced curries, fermented seafood, and southern-style pad thai that locals insist is the real version. Arrive by ferry.
_facts: Named after Rajawang Boworn Sathan Phimuk, the 'rear palace' of Prince Anurak Devesh, nephew of Rama I
Palace wall remnants survive in the area near the current market
Market extends 300 metres between Siriraj pier and Arun Amarin Road
Famous for southern Thai cuisine, particularly spicy dishes
Located on the west bank of the Chao Phraya River, opposite Tha Phra Chan pier

s: Fled from fallen Ayutthaya in 1769; Gothic Revival by the river
w: A breakaway faction of Portuguese Catholics from Ayutthaya followed their own path after that city fell in 1767 — refusing to join the French mission, settling the east bank of the Chao Phraya. They built their first church here in 1787. The current building, consecrated in 1897, is the third structure on the site: Gothic Revival in red brick with stained-glass windows considered among the finest in Thailand. The Portuguese community long since dispersed; the congregation today is mostly Thai-Chinese.
_facts: Portuguese Catholic community resettled here in 1769 after the fall of Ayutthaya, separating from the French mission
First church built in wood in 1787; second in 1838; current Gothic Revival building consecrated October 1897
Designed with a cruciform floor plan, the steeple-fronted facade faces the Chao Phraya River
Received the Association of Siamese Architects Architectural Conservation Award in 1987
Known as 'Kalawar Church' from the Portuguese Calvario; the local name refers to its Portuguese origins

s: Bangkok's first shopping district — once the city's thieves' market
w: Developed in the reign of Rama V on land granted to Prince Paribatra Sukhumbandhu, this enclave became Bangkok's first organised retail district — and also, inevitably, a clearing house for stolen goods. The name translates politely as 'delightful townspeople alcove.' In 1905, a Japanese-run zinc-roofed building here housed Siam's first cinema. Today the shophouses are mostly closing as the Paribatra estate sold the land to TCC Group for redevelopment; the signs on shuttered shops direct you to new addresses across town.
_facts: Developed during the reign of Rama V; land given to Prince Paribatra Sukhumbandhu
Regarded as Bangkok's first full-service shopping district
In 1905, housed Siam's first cinema, operated by Japanese in a temporary zinc-roofed structure
Popular nickname 'Thieves' Market' reflects its history as a trading place for stolen goods
Now partly redeveloped after TCC Group (Charoen Sirivadhanabhakdi) acquired the land

s: Italian-baroque interior behind temple walls; royal family buried within
w: Rama V built this temple between 1869 and 1887, then spent part of his reign importing European design ideas to fill it. The interior is inspired by Italian baroque architecture he encountered on visits abroad: painted ceiling, gilded pillars, mother-of-pearl inlay on the entrance doors. Behind the ordination hall is a royal cemetery containing chedis and Khmer-face towers housing the ashes of Mahidol Adulyadej, his mother Srinagarindra, and his sister Galyani Vadhana — three people still vivid in Thai memory.
_facts: Built by Rama V between 1869 and 1887 on Fueang Nakhon Road
Interior inspired by Italian baroque design seen by Rama V during European visits; gilded black lacquer doors with mother-of-pearl inlay
The 43-metre-high golden chedi is in Sri Lankan style, covered with orange-coloured tiles
Royal cemetery at the western end contains chedis with ashes of Mahidol Adulyadej, Srinagarindra (the Princess Mother), and Galyani Vadhana
The current abbot is Somdet Phra Ariyavongsagatanana, the Supreme Patriarch of Thailand

s: Prince's-palace alley rebuilt as a Sino-Portuguese food street
w: After Prince Thawee Thawalai — son of Rama IV — died in 1894, Rama V bought his palace and cut a road straight through it. The prince's name stuck. Today Phraeng Phuthon is a short lane of Sino-Portuguese shophouses, most dating to Chulalongkorn's rebuilding, that became one of the city's quiet street-food capitals: cantonese noodles, grilled pork meatballs, mango sticky rice from Kor Panich, pad thai from the now-closed Chotechitr. The lane and its neighbour Phraeng Nara form the Sam Phraeng district — 'three-way intersection,' which old Thai belief holds to be haunted.
_facts: Named after Prince Thawee Thawalai (Prince Phuthon), son of Rama IV, whose palace occupied the site until his death in 1894
Rama V bought the land and cut a road through it, building the current Sino-Portuguese shophouses
Sam Phraeng ('three-way intersection') was traditionally considered inauspicious and haunted by spirits under old Thai belief
Phraeng Nara, the neighbouring alley, was the site of the first theatre in Thailand ('Pridalai Theater'), built around 1908
Kor Panich mango sticky rice has operated on the street for over 80 years

s: The horoscope of Bangkok — buried inside at the city's founding in 1782
w: At 06:45 on Sunday 21 April 1782, Rama I buried a pillar of chaiyaphreuk (Cassia fistula) wood at this spot, enclosed in it a horoscope for the new city, and the Rattanakosin Kingdom was founded. According to the In-Chan-Mun-Kong legend, four men were buried alive at the cardinal points to serve as guardian spirits — a story the chronicles don't corroborate but which locals still retell. The original pillar was replaced in 1852; both now stand inside. The first building built in Bangkok.
_facts: Erected at 06:45 on 21 April 1782 by Rama I to mark the founding of the Rattanakosin Kingdom
The original acacia wood pillar contains a horoscope for the city of Bangkok, placed inside at founding
The In-Chan-Mun-Kong legend holds that four people were buried alive at the four cardinal points to serve as guardian spirits
The original pillar was replaced in 1852 by Rama IV with a new one; both are now preserved inside the shrine
The shrine was the first building constructed in Bangkok, built before the Grand Palace

s: French missionaries' Romanesque nave off Bangkok's oldest road
w: French missionary Father Pascal petitioned in 1809 for a church on this plot, off Oriental Avenue — the lane that would become the address of the Oriental Hotel and the French Embassy. The original building, completed 1821, was replaced by the current Romanesque red-brick structure between 1910 and 1918 after a Chinese-Teochew businessman, Low Khiok Chiang, funded most of the rebuilding. Allied bombing in 1942 damaged the surrounding buildings; the cathedral survived. It has since hosted two papal visits, by John Paul II in 1984 and Francis in 2019.
_facts: Original chapel founded 1809 by French missionary Father Pascal; completed 1821 under Rama II
Current building in Romanesque style constructed 1910-1918, funded largely by Teochew businessman Low Khiok Chiang (Jacobe)
Allied bombing in 1942 damaged nearby buildings; cathedral underwent restoration
Hosted Pope John Paul II in May 1984 and Pope Francis on 22 November 2019
Located at 23 Oriental Avenue (Soi Charoen Krung 40), next to the historic Mandarin Oriental hotel

s: Neo-Renaissance terminus where Thailand's railway began in 1916
w: The front facade was designed by Turin-born architect Mario Tamagno, who spent decades reshaping Bangkok's civic face alongside his compatriot Annibale Rigotti — they also gave the city the Ananta Samakhom Throne Hall and Bang Khun Phrom Palace. The station opened 25 June 1916, after six years of construction. In 1986, six runaway unmanned locomotives collided here, killing four. Since January 2023, long-distance trains have moved to the new Bang Sue central station; Hua Lamphong now serves short commuter routes only. Still splendid.
_facts: Opened 25 June 1916 after six years of construction under kings Rama V and Rama VI
Front designed by Turin architect Mario Tamagno; in Italian Neo-Renaissance style with wooden ceilings and stained glass
In November 1986, six runaway unmanned coupled locomotives collided at the station, killing 4 and injuring 4
On 19 January 2023, all long-distance trains were transferred to Krung Thep Aphiwat (Bang Sue) terminal
The name 'Hua Lamphong' derives from the datura plant (Lamphong) that once grew abundantly in the area

s: Teochew-funded temple whose river-front Buddha was once tallest in Bangkok
w: Chaophraya Nikonbodin — a wealthy Sino-Thai merchant — donated this temple to Rama III in 1825. The ordination hall faces directly onto the Chao Phraya, housing the massive Phra Buddha Trai Rattananayok, revered both as the Buddha and, by the ethnic Chinese, as the navigator Zheng He. The temple shares a family lineage with Wat Rakhang and Wat Chakkrawat: all three are Kalayanamitr family temples, connected by the poem inscribed here which gave the family its name. Easiest reached by river ferry from Tha Phra Chan.
_facts: Founded 1825 by Chaophraya Nikonbodin (To), a wealthy Thai-Chinese merchant, donated to Rama III
The main Phra Buddha Trai Rattananayok image, known as Luang Pho To, was once considered the largest Buddha in Bangkok
The image is revered by both Buddhists as the Buddha and by Chinese as Sampokong (Zheng He, the admiral)
One of three family temples of the Kalayanamitr clan, alongside Wat Rakhang and Wat Chakkrawat
The family surname Kalayanamitr derives from a poem inscribed at this temple

s: Golden-domed Sikh temple in Bangkok's Little India
w: Phahurat Road became Bangkok's South Asian quarter after Sikh merchants from Punjab settled here in the late 19th century, establishing a textile trading enclave that has outlasted most of what surrounded it. The golden-domed Siri Guru Singh Sabha — the main Sikh temple in Thailand — rises above a district still dominated by Indian fabric shops, South Asian restaurants, and garland sellers. The street takes its name from Princess Phahurat Manimaya, daughter of Rama V, who died young; the name derives from the Sanskrit for 'gold upper arm bracelet.'
_facts: Sikh merchants from Punjab began settling in Phahurat in the late 19th century
The Siri Guru Singh Sabha is the main Sikh gurdwara in Thailand
Phahurat Road was named by King Chulalongkorn after his daughter Princess Phahurat Manimaya, who died young
The name 'Phahurat' derives from Sanskrit for 'gold upper arm bracelet'
The area was previously home to Annamese (Vietnamese) immigrants during the reign of King Taksin

s: Hundreds of carved phalluses; Bangkok's most unusual fertility shrine
w: Nai Lert, one of early 20th-century Bangkok's most powerful businessmen, found a spirit house floating in Khlong Saen Saep and placed it on the bank of his property — an act of piety that grew into this. Women hoping to conceive bring offerings to Chao Mae Tuptim, a pre-Buddhist tree spirit, including carved wooden phalluses that now number in the hundreds, ranging from palm-sized to canoe-sized. The site is tucked behind what is now a luxury hotel on Wireless Road. Earnest, abundant, and utterly unlike anything else in the city.
_facts: Created by businessman Nai Lert (1872-1945) in the early 20th century after he found a spirit house in the canal
The shrine is dedicated to Chao Mae Tuptim, a pre-Buddhist Southeast Asian tree spirit
Worshippers, mostly women seeking fertility or pregnancy, donate carved wooden phalluses; over 100 are now on site
Located behind the Mövenpick BDMS Wellness Resort Bangkok on Wireless Road
Considered one of the best examples of intentional phallic religious architecture in the world

s: Last neighbourhood in Bangkok trading joss paper and ghost money
w: The first colonial-style shophouses on Soi Charoen Chai 1 and 2 went up in 1898 under Rama V, settled by Cantonese migrants and later Teochew. The community became the main wholesale source of Chinese ritual goods for Bangkok — joss paper, spirit money, moon-festival offerings, traditional wedding items — and has remained so for over a century while everything around it changed. The Charoen Chai Community Museum opened in 2011 in a restored shophouse. The alley name means 'prosperity-luck.'
_facts: First shophouses built in 1898 on Soi Charoen Chai 1 and 2 in the Pom Prap Sattru Phai district
Originally settled by Cantonese immigrants, later joined by Teochew
Community is Bangkok's largest and oldest marketplace for Chinese joss paper and ritual goods
In 2015 the community comprised about 60 shophouses and 80 households
The Baan Kao Lao Rueng (Charoen Chai Community Museum) opened in 2011

s: Chulalongkorn drew the route himself; birthplace of Thai-Chinese fortunes
w: After a fire cleared the Sampheng district in 1892, King Chulalongkorn personally drew the route of a new riverside road on a map with a pencil — the name Song Wat means 'drawn by the king.' The 1.2-kilometre street became the main cargo pier access for steamships connecting Bangkok to the provinces, and the founding address of several of Thailand's largest Thai-Chinese business dynasties, including Chearavanont and Sirivadhanabhakdi. Song Soem, the shortest road in Thailand at 20 metres, branches off to the river.
_facts: Built in 1892 following a fire in Sampheng; Rama V personally drew the route on a map, giving the road its name ('drawn by the king')
Served as the main access route to cargo piers for steamships connecting Bangkok to coastal provinces
Origin of several major Thai-Chinese business dynasties including Chearavanont (CP Group) and Sirivadhanabhakdi
Song Soem Road, a 20-metre branch off Song Wat, is considered the shortest road in Thailand
Named 39th coolest neighbourhood in the world by Time Out magazine in 2023

s: Thailand's first land market, 1900; rare Thai sweets still sold daily
w: Rama V opened this market on 29 March 1900, the first purpose-built land market in the country — before it, the Mon people traded here by boat on the canal. The name derives from I Loeng, a Mon jar, softened under later governance to the more polite Nang Loeng. The surrounding shophouses are colonial-style, most original to Chulalongkorn's era. The central shrine honours Prince Abhakara Kiartivongse, who had close ties to the early market community. Thai desserts are still the speciality: kanom farang, thong yib, thong yod.
_facts: Officially opened 29 March 1900 by King Chulalongkorn (Rama V) as Thailand's first purpose-built land market
Name derives from 'I Loeng', a Mon jar used in canal trading; changed to the more polite 'Nang Loeng' under later governance
The original area around the market was called Ban Sanam Khwai, meaning 'a place for raising buffaloes'
The central shrine is dedicated to Prince Abhakara Kiartivongse, who had personal connections to the market community
Known for rare traditional Thai desserts; a closed cinema (Sala Chaloem Thani, 1993) is now used as a warehouse

s: One of two surviving forts from Bangkok's 1782 city walls
w: Rama I built fourteen forts along the Rattanakosin walls in 1782; of the fourteen, only two remain. Phra Sumen, at the northernmost corner where the moat met the river, is an octagonal masonry drum 45 metres across and 10.5 metres tall, with a heptagonal roofed tower rebuilt in 1981 after it collapsed sometime between Rama V and Rama VII. The surrounding grounds became Santichaiprakarn Park, named by King Bhumibol, where the last lamphu tree in Bangkok grows — the tree whose name gave Bang Lamphu its name.
_facts: Built by Rama I in 1782 as one of fourteen defensive forts along the Rattanakosin city walls
Octagonal masonry structure, 45 metres in diameter and 10.5 metres tall; the original heptagonal tower collapsed between the reigns of Rama V and Rama VII
Tower rebuilt in 1981 to celebrate the 200th anniversary of Bangkok's founding
Grounds developed into Santichaiprakarn Park in 1999, named by King Bhumibol Adulyadej
The only remaining lamphu tree (Sonneratia caseolaris) in Bangkok grows in the park — the tree that gave Bang Lamphu its name

s: Last community inside Bangkok's original city walls fought eviction for years
w: The second of the two surviving Rattanakosin forts stands at the eastern edge of the old island city, where Khlong Maha Nak meets the outer moat — a three-tiered octagon, 38 metres across and 19 metres tall. Between the fort and the canal wall, an old wooden community clung on for decades in vernacular teak houses that are among the last of their kind in the historic core. The Bangkok Metropolitan Administration and the community fought a long public eviction battle; the final residents were removed in 2018. The houses remain, now museum pieces.
_facts: Built by Rama I in 1782 as part of the Rattanakosin fortifications; three-tiered octagonal fort, 38m diameter and 19m tall
Situated at the junction of Khlong Maha Nak and the outer moat at Phan Fa Lilat Bridge
A community of wooden houses between the fort and the canal survived for over 200 years
The community engaged in a decades-long legal and public dispute against eviction by the Bangkok Metropolitan Administration
The last residents were removed in 2018; the wooden houses remain as an open-air heritage site
